did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) framework integrated with Explainable AI (XAI) for automated skin cancer classification. The method employs deep learning architectures including DenseNet, EfficientNet, and ResNet to classify melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancers using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) framework integrated with Explainable AI (XAI) for automated skin cancer classification. It details the proposed model's architecture (integrating DenseNet, EfficientNet, and ResNet), describes the experimental methodology using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets, and reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 85.5% accuracy on HAM10000, 87.2% on ISIC 2018). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICESIC 2026), it constitutes original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: Transparent Multi-View Learning Framework for Automated Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) framework integrated with Explainable AI (XAI) for the automated classification of melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancers. The model leverages deep learning architectures, specifically DenseNet, EfficientNet, and ResNet, to extract and aggregate features at multiple semantic levels from dermoscopic images. The proposed method was trained and evaluated using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 public datasets, with performance assessed via accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC metrics. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed model achieved an accuracy of 85.5% on the HAM10000 dataset and 87.2% on the ISIC 2018 dataset. These results indicate that the HMVFA framework outperforms baseline CNN models and other state-of-the-art technologies in classifying skin lesions. The integration of XAI mechanisms, such as Grad-CAM and LIME, further enhances the model's interpretability and robustness for clinical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification; labeled as '1' in the binary classification task. The paper distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma in the algorithm description (Chunk 11).

Secondary target class for classification; labeled as '0' in the binary classification task. The paper notes that non-melanoma skin cancers include squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) (Chunk 2) and lists Basal cell carcinoma and Actinic keratoses as examples present in the HAM10000 dataset (Chunk 11).
Mentioned in the introduction as a cause of non-melanoma skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label: Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma skin cancer
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin lesions into 'Melanoma' (labeled as 1) and 'Non-melanoma' (labeled as 0). The model uses deep learning architectures (DenseNet, EfficientNet, ResNet) to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public thermoscopic (dermoscopic) image dataset composed of 10,015 images covering 7 different types of skin lesion images, including melanoma, non-melanoma, Basal cell carcinoma, and Actinic keratoses.

use.training: Used for training the proposed Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) model (80% of the data).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions 80% used for training and 20% for testing.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed model (20% of the data).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public thermoscopic image dataset composed of 10,000 images, used for lesion segmentation and disease classification.

use.training: Used for training the proposed Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) model (80% of the data).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions 80% used for training and 20% for testing.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed model (20% of the data).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.) without statistical significance analysis.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model was trained on 80% of the data and tested on 20%. It outperformed baseline DL, BTG, AI, and ML models.

ISIC 2018.notes: The proposed model was trained on 80% of the data and tested on 20%. It outperformed baseline DL, BTG, AI, and ML models.

architecture_used.main_model: Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) framework integrated with Explainable AI (XAI)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hierarchical multi-view feature extraction capturing low, medium, and high-level features from multiple semantic levels
architecture_details.feature_aggregation: Alignment of feature maps to common dimensions (h,w) followed by fusion using weighted sum and concatenation: F_fused = concat(F_hat_Low, F_hat_Mid, F_hat_High)
architecture_details.pooling: Global Average Pooling (GAP) to reduce feature map to a single vector z_f
architecture_details.xai_integration: Grad-CAM and LIME mechanisms for visual explanations and transparency

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (Melanoma vs Non-melanoma) via arg max of softmax probabilities
classification_or_output_head.explainability: Grad-CAM heatmap generation using gradient averaging over spatial locations

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Dense Group Shuffle Non-Local (DGSNLA) attention network
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Input images resized to 224x224x3 dimensions to align with CNN backbones (DenseNet, ResNet).
Normalization: Pixel-wise normalization applied per channel (R, G, B) using mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ) as per Equation 3.
Artifact Suppression: Explicitly listed as part of the pre-processing stage.
Image Enhancement: Applied to enhance lesion visibility using gain factor (γ) and gamma correction exponent (α) as per Equation 4.
Data Augmentation: Stochastic transformation process applied to obtain final processed images as per Equation 5.
Feature Map Alignment: Feature maps from different semantic levels (low, mid, high) resized to common spatial dimensions (h, w) for fusion.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a data augmentation process to obtain final processed images, described by Equation 5 which includes resizing, normalization, and image enhancement operations.

conclusions: The proposed Hierarchical Multi-View Feature Aggregation (HMVFA) framework integrated with Explainable AI (XAI) outperforms baseline Deep Learning (DL), Banyan Tree Growth optimization (BTG), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Machine Learning (ML) models in classifying melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancers.
The model achieved an accuracy of 85.5% on the HAM10000 dataset and 87.2% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
On the HAM10000 dataset, the proposed model achieved a precision of 83.1%, recall of 82.0%, specificity of 88.7%, F1 score of 82.5%, and AUC-ROC of 0.89.
On the ISIC 2018 dataset, the proposed model achieved a precision of 85.7%, recall of 84.4%, specificity of 89.9%, F1 score of 85.0%, and AUC-ROC of 0.91.

The integration of low, mid, and high-level features from CNN architectures (ResNet, EfficientNet, and DenseNet) enriches representation capacity and ensures no critical discriminative features are missed.
The use of XAI mechanisms (Grad-CAM and LIME) enhances the interpretability and trustworthiness of the model for real-time clinical applications.
The model exhibits minimal false positives and false negatives, thereby enhancing precision, recall, and specificity.

The proposed lightweight model enables fast and accurate diagnosis, with potential for generalization across various skin tones, capturing devices, and lighting conditions due to training on public datasets.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma), ignoring the clinical distinction between benign and malignant melanoma classes present in the HAM10000 dataset.
The model relies exclusively on thermoscopic/RGB images, explicitly ignoring valuable clinical metadata, thermal images, and multi-modal data sources.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The paper does not report per-class sample counts or address class imbalance within the training data, despite acknowledging imbalance as a general challenge in the field.
External validation is lacking; the model was only evaluated on two public datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2018) without testing on independent, multi-center clinical cohorts.
The data split is reported as a simple 80/20 train-test split without mention of cross-validation or stratified sampling, raising potential concerns about result stability.
The paper claims generalization across various skin tones and lighting conditions but provides no specific demographic breakdown or bias analysis of the training data.

The proposed model's accuracy (85.5% on HAM10000) is lower than some cited state-of-the-art methods in the literature review (e.g., 94.21% accuracy by Karthik et al.), suggesting the baseline comparisons may not represent the current best performance.
The term 'thermoscopic' is used incorrectly to describe dermoscopic images, indicating potential terminology errors in the manuscript.
The computational complexity and inference time of the multi-view aggregation framework are not reported, limiting the assessment of its 'lightweight' claim for real-time deployment.
